name: Sanitation and Clean Water
tagline: Safe drinking water, waste isolation, and hygiene habits that prevent waterborne disease when infrastructure disappears
problem:
Human beings are walking conflict zones for microbes: every litre of surface water near settlements is a potential culture medium seeded by faeces, urine, decaying biomass, and biofilms that protect pathogens from simple fixes. In the first hours after disruption, people still behave as if taps and chlorine exist; within days, diarrhoeal disease can remove more working adults from a labour pool than injury or violence. Children dehydrate fastest; caregivers then face impossible trade-offs between fetching water and staying at a bedside.

The second failure mode is engineering without discipline. A community can build an impressive filter or a gleaming boiling kettle and still poison itself by dipping unwashed hands into storage, by rinsing dishes in untreated stream water, or by locating a latrine uphill of the spring because the downhill slope was rockier to dig. Water safety is a system: source selection, transport, treatment, storage, and waste must be designed as one loop, not five independent hobbies.

The third failure mode is chemical complacency. Boiling does not remove arsenic, nitrates from agricultural runoff, or many industrial solvents. Clear water is not innocent water. Teaching people to recognise the limits of each method — heat, sand, sunlight, measured halogens — is as important as teaching them to tie knots.

On Mars or in closed habitats, water is the primary life-support fluid. Every litre is part of a closed-loop system where cross-contamination between greywater (wash), blackwater (waste), and potable storage is an existential threat.
overview:
Practical field water treatment stacks independent barriers. First, protect the source: choose headwater springs or rain from clean roofs where possible; fence livestock away. Second, reduce turbidity: settle in a series of jars or a drum, decanting progressively cleaner supernatant; pre-filter through cloth, then through packed sand and gravel.

Third, disinfect: a rolling boil for one minute (three minutes at altitudes >2000m) inactivates most bacterial and viral threats. Solar disinfection (SODIS) uses UV-A radiation and modest heating (above 50°C) in clear PET bottles laid in full sun for 6-48 hours. Where halogen chemistry exists, measured contact time (typically 30 minutes) is required for chlorine or iodine to work.

Sanitation is the mirror image of drinking water: isolate human waste from fingers, flies, feet, and wind. Deep pit latrines (minimum 2m depth) with fly-proof covers, trench latrines rotated on a schedule, and meticulous separation of greywater from potable storage break the faecal-oral routes. Hand hygiene with soap removes a staggering fraction of risk; where soap is absent, wood ash paste (alkaline) and friction still outperform hope.

On Mars, the 'Sanitation' challenge is brine management: separating salts and perchlorates from regolith ice to produce potable water, while recycling all habitat waste via anaerobic digesters or high-pressure oxidation.

principles.name: Multi-barrier protection
principles.explanation: Settling, filtration, disinfection, and safe storage each remove different failure classes; redundancy (e.g., filtering AND boiling) is the field standard.

principles.name: Schmutzdecke (Biofilm)
principles.explanation: In slow-sand filters, the top 2cm of sand hosts a biological layer of predatory microbes that eat pathogens. A filter is not 'mature' until this layer develops (typically 14-21 days).

principles.name: Turbidity shielding
principles.explanation: Microscopic particles (clay/silt) act as 'shields' for bacteria against UV radiation and chemical disinfectants. Water MUST be clear before the final kill step.

principles.name: Hydraulic gradient
principles.explanation: Groundwater and surface flows follow gravity; latrines must be at least 30 meters away and down-gradient from any water intake.

principles.name: Perchlorate toxicity (Mars)
principles.explanation: Mars regolith contains perchlorates (salts) that disrupt thyroid function. Water extracted from ice must be treated via ion exchange or reverse osmosis to remove these chemicals.

components.name: Protected intake
components.function: Collects water with minimal sediment disturbance
components.madeFrom: Screened pipe, bamboo, or ceramic conduit
components.criticalNote: Position intake mid-depth — avoid surface scum and bottom silt

components.name: Settling vessels / Flocculation
components.function: Gravity separation of sand and organic floc
components.madeFrom: Matched jars or drums; use crushed Moringa seeds or Alum to speed settling
components.criticalNote: Discard sludge away from the intake zone

components.name: Biosand filter
components.function: Removes particles; mature slow-sand bed hosts biocidal biofilm
components.madeFrom: Washed river sand (0.1-0.7mm), pea gravel support, outlet valve
components.criticalNote: Never allow the sand surface to dry; maintain 5cm of standing water

components.name: Boiling kettle
components.criticalNote: Boiling kills everything biological but concentrates chemical toxins through evaporation

components.name: SODIS bottles
components.function: UV and thermal disinfection using sunlight
components.position: Full sun exposure on a reflective surface (e.g., corrugated metal)
components.criticalNote: Discard bottles when they become scratched or opaque

components.name: Pit latrine with cover
components.position: Down-gradient from wells; minimum 2m above water table
components.madeFrom: Deep pit, concrete or wood slab, fly screen vent
components.criticalNote: Add a cup of wood ash after each use to reduce odor and speed decomposition

rawMaterials.name: Source water
earthLocations: Headwater springs, deep tested wells, rain from clean metal/tile roofs (avoid lead flashing)
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Mars: subsurface glacier ice or hydrated clay minerals; recycle urine/condensate via membrane distillation.
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Pre-screen for heavy metals if near mining sites; boiling will NOT remove lead or mercury

rawMaterials.name: Filter media
earthLocations: Sharp river sand (sieved), pea gravel (washed and boiled)
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Sintered regolith beads or basalt-fibre mesh filters
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Wash sand until the water runs clear; sterilise with boiling water during initial setup

rawMaterials.name: Disinfectant or fuel
rawMaterials.purpose: Final kill step or heat source
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Electrolytic chlorine generators or UVC (254nm) lamps
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Calculate 'CT' (Concentration x Time) based on water temperature and pH

buildSteps.title: Watershed audit and hazard map
buildSteps.description: Walk 1km upstream of your intake. Identify animal feedlots, burials, or chemical dumps. Mark a 'fetch route' that is kept clear of human waste and mud.
buildSteps.warningNote: Clear water does not mean safe water — assume all surface water is contaminated until treated

buildSteps.title: The Decant Chain (Settling)
buildSteps.description: Fill 'Raw' vessel. Wait 12-24 hours. Decant the top 80% into 'Settled' vessel. Use a clean cloth over the rim. This step removes the majority of parasites (like Cryptosporidium) that are attached to silt.
buildSteps.warningNote: Do not stir the bottom dregs; they contain the highest pathogen concentration

buildSteps.title: Filter commissioning
buildSteps.description: Pack your biosand filter. Run 50L of water through it and discard. Feed it with settled water daily to allow the 'Schmutzdecke' to grow. Test with a clear glass — water must be crystal clear before moving to step 4.
buildSteps.warningNote: A new filter is only a mechanical strainer; it is NOT biocide-active for the first 2 weeks

buildSteps.title: The Kill Step (Boil or SODIS)
buildSteps.description: Boil for 1 minute (rolling). If using SODIS, place bottles in sun for 6 hours (sunny) or 2 days (cloudy). The water must reach 50°C and receive UV-A exposure. Storage must be in the same bottle or a sterile container.
buildSteps.warningNote: If using SODIS, bottles MUST be clear plastic; glass blocks necessary UV-A wavelengths

buildSteps.title: Latrine and hygiene station
buildSteps.description: Dig a 2m pit. Build a sealed platform with a 'drop-hole' that can be covered. Install a 'Tippy-Tap' (suspended jug) for handwashing with soap or ash. Enforce the rule: wash hands BEFORE fetching water.
buildSteps.warningNote: Never locate a latrine within 30m of a well; ensure the floor is non-slip to prevent falls into the pit

history.event: Cisterns, qanats, and aqueducts distribute water across dry civilisations.
Cholera outbreak mapping links water source to disease — modern field epidemiology begins.
The Biosand filter is adapted from industrial slow-sand technology for household use in the developing world.

inventors.name: John Snow
inventors.contribution: Father of modern epidemiology; proved waterborne transmission of cholera

impact:
Communities that keep drinking water and faeces apart survive long enough to argue about laws, calendars, and machines. Communities that fail this lesson disappear quietly into fever charts.

Space habitats inherit the same moral: there is no dilution sink — only engineering and rigorous procedure.

images.caption: Cross-section of a biosand filter showing sand, schmutzdecke, and gravel layers

videos.title: SODIS water disinfection
videos.description: How to use sunlight to make water safe; follow time and temperature requirements strictly.

warnings: Chemical toxins (lead/arsenic) are NOT removed by boiling
CO2/CO accumulation during indoor water boiling

sources.note: The global gold standard for water safety and treatment
